retrieved_docs.title: Dental Mini-Implants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Since ancient times, it has been a challenge to come up with the best way to replace missing teeth. Previously, dentures were the standard way of replacing lost teeth. Science, technology, and researchers have provided choices for better care of teeth and understanding of oral health, leading to solutions for most oral problems. Rehabilitation of the stomatognathic system includes the restoration of normal contour, function, esthetics, comfort, speech, and health. [1] Osseointegration has become the focus of modern implantology, leading to the introduction and refinement of the osseointegrated root form implant. Available implants vary in diameter from 1.8 mm to 7 mm. The mini implant is a dental implant that is fabricated with a reduced diameter (less than 3 mm) and a shorter length but with the same biocompatible material as compared with standard dental implants. Mini implants present a reduced diameter (less than 3 mm), while narrow/conventional diameter implants typically have a diameter greater than 3 mm. Therefore, the use of mini implants to retain overdentures enables the use of less-complex surgical techniques since the reduced diameter of the implant permits its placement in areas with low bone thickness. These implants are associated with immediate stability, high survival rates, favorable marginal bone loss, less postoperative discomfort, and increased satisfaction and quality of life of patients. The quantity and quality of bone tissue available in the jaw typically define the characteristics (diameter and length) and the number of implants. Overdentures retained by conventional implants exhibit good long-term results, but also present some limitations such as cost, difficulty with placing the implant in reduced buccolingual dimensions of bone without the need for bone-grafting procedures, and the presence of chronic systemic diseases that can prevent most advanced surgeries such as bone grafts and lateralization of the inferior alveolar nerve. Concomitantly, sometimes it is not necessary to open flaps, decreasing morbidity during the postoperative period. Additional advantages include bone expansion while placement requiring minimal osteotomy size leading to the extra available osseous blood supply to the supporting bone and better angiogenesis. Also easy removal and healing in case of failure with minimal surgical trauma. [1] Excellent patient satisfaction was found while evaluating patients using overdentures supported with mini implants in terms of comfort, retention, chewing ability, and speaking ability. [2] These aspects are some of the attractive factors that increase patient acceptance of mini-implant treatments. [3] [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Despite these disadvantages, the need for mini implants will continue to grow, especially among edentulous patients because of the following: An increase in the need for complete dentures The increased cost of standard implants Access-to-care issues, especially among economically disadvantaged patients and patients indicated for maxillofacial prostheses Medically compromised patients who may not be candidates for traditional surgical procedures or ridge augmentation procedures Use of mini implants as transitional implants to support prosthesis during the healing phase of implant denture restoration. [22] As an alternative to the standard implants, when inadequate space and limited bone is available, a mini implant-supported permanent single crown can be placed [23] Increased interest in implant dentistry among general dentists

retrieved_docs.content: Organized dentistry has finally accepted implant dentistry. The current trend to expand the use of implant dentistry will continue until every restorative practice uses this modality for abutment support of both fixed and removable prostheses on a regular basis as the primary option for all tooth replacements.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: When making a treatment plan for a questionable tooth, many factors come into play to decide whether the tooth should be treated and maintained, or extracted and replaced by an implant. From a periodontal standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of attachment loss and probing pocket depth. The progressive loss of periodontal attachment and increase in probe pocket depth are predictive of additional disease activity increases the likelihood of needing to be extracted. If the bone loss is too great with the periodontal loss, an implant would only be a possible replacement with successful bone volume augmentation. From a tooth structural standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of coronal structure remaining. A prosthetic crown can be placed on the tooth if there is sufficient structure remaining to retain the crown. Crown lengthening and extrusion can increase the amount of tooth structure for sufficient retention of a dental crown when appropriate. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: People are living longer on average. This fact, combined with an existing population of patients with minor and major dental problems, guarantees the future of implant dentistry for several generations of dentists. Dental implants are increasingly used to replace single teeth, especially in the posterior regions of the mouth. Rather than removing sound tooth structure and crowning two or more teeth, increasing the risk of decay, endodontic therapy, and splinting teeth together with pontics, which may have the potential to decrease oral hygiene ability and increase plaque retention, a dental implant may replace the single tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Mini-Implants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Therefore, the current evidence must be reviewed and synthesized with the available clinical data on the survival of mini implants for definitive prosthodontic treatment. Following factors influence the clinical success of mini implants: 1. Mini implant design: Improvement of implant shape, thread patterns and surface treatments lead to enhanced primary stability and faster osseointegration [24] [25] 2. Size of mini-implant effects the area of possible retention in bones [1] 3. Favorable occlusion and masticatory forces significantly influence mini-implant success [26] [27] 4. Number and position of mini-implant within the prosthesis affect the forces acting on the bone surrounding the implants [26] [27] 5. The stress distribution is influenced by implant diameter and shape along with the load direction [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A dental implant is one of the treatments to replace missing teeth. Their use in the treatment of complete and partial edentulism has become an integral treatment modality in dentistry. Dental implants have a number of advantages over conventional fixed partial denture. A high success rate (above 97% for 10 years) A decreased risk of caries and endodontic problems of adjacent teeth Improved maintenance of bone in edentulous site Decreased sensitivity of adjacent teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The goal of modern dentistry is to restore the patient to normal profile, function, comfort, esthetics, speech, and health regardless of the atrophy, disease, or injury of the stomatognathic system.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: It is generally accepted that dental implant treatment is done for restorative purposes. The importance of presurgical communication and cooperation between the restorative dentist, periodontist, dental technician, and the implant surgeon is well recognized in modern implantology. The predictable outcome of function and aesthetics of intraoral rehabilitation can and must be determined and controlled before the surgical procedure, with the entire restorative team (the periodontist, surgeon, restorative dentist, laboratory, and patient). Competent interprofessional team-work is essential for the successful completion of implant-retained restorations. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Stability in Mandibular Denture -- Issues of Concern -- B. Residual Ridge Factor
retrieved_docs.content: Ridge resorption can result in a prominent and sharp mylohyoid ridge, limiting the denture extension below the mylohyoid area, potentially affecting denture stability. Mylohyoid ridge reductions can aid in the success of mandibular denture by permitting the increased surface area coverage by the denture. In highly resorbed ridges with shallow sulcus depth, adequate stability is not achievable through non-surgical procedures. Pre-prosthetic surgery measures, including sulcus deepening and/or ridge augmentation procedures, are to be adopted to ensure the stability of the mandibular denture.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Implants -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common postoperative complication is incision line opening. The design of the removable interim prosthesis is involved, it is corrected. The patient is instructed to rinse 2-3 times daily with chlorhexidine. If granulation process extends for more than two weeks, epithelial margin trimming can be done. If implants become exposed during the healing period, no attempt should be made to cover them with tissue. Rather denture is relieved aggressively over the area with implant exposure. The mobility of the implant during healing is unusual but may occur, mostly accompanied by a radiolucent zone around the implant. Whatever may be the cause, the implant should be removed. Signs and symptoms of failure for an implant are horizontal mobility  greater than 0.5 mm, rapid progressive bone loss, pain during percussion, uncontrolled exudate, generalized radiolucency around the implant, more than one half of the bone is lost around the implant and last the implants inserted in poor position, making them useless for prosthetic support. A success rate of 85%at the end of 5 year period and 80% at the end of 10 year period are minimum criteria for success. [15] [16] [17] [18]

This classification highlights that both activities are integral components of prosthetic rehabilitation, aimed at ensuring proper device fit and functionality for optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a surgical procedure that encompasses distinct child operations, specifically بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation) and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These two levels of amputation represent different anatomical sites of limb removal, each influencing postoperative function, prosthetic fitting, and rehabilitation strategies for the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is a specific therapeutic intervention performed on joints. It is categorized under the broader group of other or therapeutic procedures on joints, indicating that it is one of several surgical options aimed at treating joint pathology. Additionally, Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is identified as a type of بضع المفصل, linking it to the general concept of joint removal procedures. This classification highlights its role within the spectrum of joint surgeries and underscores its relevance for clinicians selecting appropriate operative strategies for prosthetic complications or severe joint disease.

This activity involves the precise measurement, fitting, and subsequent adjustment of external prosthetic devices that have not been otherwise specified, ensuring optimal function and comfort for the patient. By situating this procedure under the broader category of rehabilitation care; fitting of prostheses; and adjustment of devices, it highlights its role in restoring mobility, enhancing independence, and improving overall quality of life for individuals requiring prosthetic support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes several more specific procedures. Within this category, بتر أصبع القدم refers to the amputation of an individual toe, بتر القدم denotes the removal of the entire foot, and بتر فوق الركبة describes an amputation performed above the knee joint. These distinct levels of lower‑extremity amputation differ in the extent of tissue removed, functional impact, and rehabilitation requirements, and recognizing each as a child of the general amputation of lower extremity concept aids clinicians in planning appropriate surgical approaches, postoperative care, and prosthetic fitting for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1992
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). A notable complication associated with these procedures is necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb, which is directly linked to the amputation of lower extremity. This necrotic condition is characterized by the morphological finding النَخَر (necrosis). The relationship between lower‑extremity amputation and subsequent stump necrosis underscores the importance of monitoring for tissue viability and managing potential complications in patients undergoing toe or below‑knee amputations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Prosthetic and joint‑related interventions involve “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate,” “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure),” and “Trochanterplasty,” which address femoral implant revision, comprehensive knee arthroplasty component exchange, and greater trochanter reshaping to improve hip biomechanics. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the extensive, femur‑centered surgical repertoire for managing growth disturbances, fractures, malunions, deformities, and prosthetic complications, emphasizing the need for individualized operative planning based on anatomical site, underlying pathology, and desired functional outcome.

Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). Together, these relationships show that hip neck fractures, their surgical management (internal fixation or prosthetic replacement), and the occurrence of multiple fractures are all recognized as clinical manifestations or treatments associated with osteoporosis/low bone density, underscoring the importance of bone health assessment and appropriate orthopedic intervention in patients with compromised skeletal integrity.
